facts.label: Minimum approved tank size
facts.note: Georgia's homeowner guide says the minimum approved tank size is 1,000 gallons and ties that baseline to a 3- or 4-bedroom house.

facts.label: Public flow anchor
facts.note: Georgia's public homeowner material uses 150 gallons per bedroom per day for a single-family residence.

facts.label: Garbage disposal rule
facts.note: Georgia is unusually explicit here: the homeowner guide says homes with garbage disposals need a septic tank that is 50 percent larger.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.note: Georgia homeowners usually move from the estimate into county environmental health review, not a one-size statewide permit desk.

facts.label: Public sizing example
facts.note: Pennsylvania DEP's homeowner page uses 900 gallons or larger as the public example for a 3-bedroom home.

facts.label: Primary sizing basis
facts.sourceSection: How septic tank size is set
facts.note: DEP tells homeowners tank sizes are generally based on the number of bedrooms in the home.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: Municipality or local agency and Sewage Enforcement Officer
facts.note: Pennsylvania is strong for local workflow content because DEP makes the municipality or local agency and the Sewage Enforcement Officer central to the actual process.

facts.label: Local SEO lookup
facts.sourceSection: Active Sewage Enforcement Officers by county
facts.note: The state publishes an active SEO directory by county, which is one reason Pennsylvania pages convert well into local next steps.

facts.label: Residential design flow
facts.note: Connecticut public guidance uses 150 gallons per bedroom per day for residential design sewage flow.

facts.label: Potential-bedroom risk
facts.note: Connecticut is unusual because potential bedrooms and change-in-use risk can matter even when current occupancy is lower.

facts.label: Reserve area and additions
facts.note: Building additions and changes in use are tied to code-complying area and reserve area concerns, which is why Connecticut estimates should stay conservative.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: Local director of health or approved agent
facts.sourceSection: Approval to construct and discharge procedures
facts.note: The local director of health or approved agent controls the practical next step once a homeowner moves beyond broad online sizing logic.

facts.label: First official step
facts.note: Oregon is one of the clearest states where site evaluation comes before permit certainty, which is why tank-size-only answers need extra caution.

facts.label: What site evaluation does not do
facts.value: Does not guarantee a specific system type
facts.note: Oregon DEQ says the site evaluation does not guarantee approval of a specific system type, so online sizing estimates must stay broader.

facts.label: Records lookup path
facts.note: Oregon is strong for records-first workflow because DEQ provides an official path to locate septic system records online.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.note: After the estimate, Oregon homeowners usually need the local onsite authority directory more than a generic statewide article.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.note: California's OWTS Policy explicitly authorizes local agencies such as county environmental health departments to issue OWTS permits.

facts.label: Program structure
facts.note: California homeowners can land in the default Tier 1 path or a Regional Water Board-approved LAMP, which is why county-level routing matters so much.

facts.label: LAMP variation risk
facts.value: LAMP standards may differ from Tier 1
facts.note: California's approved LAMPs can use local siting and design standards that differ from the default Tier 1 program.

facts.label: Where to verify locally
facts.value: Regional board and local agency contact list
facts.note: California publishes a current OWTS regional-jurisdiction and local-agency contact list, which is the right first stop before trusting a quote.

facts.label: Permit requirement
facts.note: Texas states plainly that a permit and approved plan are required before most OSSF construction, alteration, repair, extension, or operation.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.note: Texas homeowners need the local permitting authority quickly because TCEQ says local programs can be more stringent than state minimums.

facts.label: Permit review window
facts.note: Texas says the permitting authority must approve or deny the permit application and planning materials within 30 days of receipt.

facts.label: First technical step
facts.note: Texas requires a site evaluation by a licensed site evaluator or professional engineer before the real system choice becomes clear.

facts.label: Local authority lookup
facts.note: Texas OARS is useful for homeowners because TCEQ says it gives the general public contact information for local permitting authorities.

facts.label: Conventional pumping recommendation
facts.note: Texas recommends pumping conventional systems every three to five years, while also warning that some permitting authorities may impose stricter maintenance rules.

facts.label: Residential scope
facts.note: New York's Appendix 75-A applies to residential onsite wastewater treatment systems serving properties under 1,000 gallons per day.

facts.label: Minimum daily flow
facts.note: New York's Appendix 75-A says new construction is based on a minimum daily flow of 110 gallons per bedroom.

facts.label: Specific waiver path
facts.value: State or designated local health official
facts.note: Appendix 75-A allows specific waivers to be issued by the State Commissioner of Health or a designated city county or part-county health department official.

facts.label: Primary local path
facts.value: District office or county health department
facts.sourceSection: Field offices and local health departments
facts.note: New York publishes a field-office and local-health-department directory because homeowners still need the right district office or county health department on the file.

facts.label: Program scope
facts.sourceSection: Who regulates household sewage treatment systems?
facts.note: Ohio EPA's homeowner FAQ says household sewage treatment systems serving one-, two-, or three-family dwellings are regulated under Chapter 3701-29 through the Ohio Department of Health.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.sourceSection: Who regulates household sewage treatment systems?
facts.note: Ohio EPA says local health departments are responsible for permitting, code enforcement, nuisance investigations, and operational inspections for household sewage systems.

facts.label: Permit requirement
facts.note: Chapter 3701-29 includes installation-permit and operation-permit requirements for HSTS and SFOSTS work, which is why Ohio homeowners need the local permit file early.

facts.label: Off-lot discharge wrinkle
facts.note: Ohio EPA says household sewage treatment systems that discharge off lot may need NPDES general permit coverage, even though the local health department remains the first point of contact.

facts.label: Program structure
facts.value: Required service for local health departments
facts.note: EGLE describes onsite wastewater management as a required service for local health departments under the Michigan Public Health Code.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: Local health departments permit and inspect
facts.note: Michigan's environmental regulations guide says the onsite sanitary wastewater program is administered in coordination with local health departments that permit and inspect systems.

facts.label: If failure is suspected
facts.sourceSection: What should I do if I suspect my septic system has failed
facts.note: EGLE's public septic FAQ tells homeowners to contact the local health department if they suspect the system has failed.

facts.label: If system location is unclear
facts.sourceSection: I need to know where my septic system is located
facts.note: EGLE's public septic FAQ tells homeowners to contact the local health department if they are unsure where the septic system is located.

facts.label: Local variation risk
facts.value: Communities may add local ordinance requirements
facts.note: Michigan's environmental regulations guide notes that some communities may have local ordinance requirements in addition to the statewide framework.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: ADEQ delegated authority to all 15 counties
facts.note: ADEQ says it has delegated permitting authority for onsite wastewater treatment facilities to all 15 Arizona counties.

facts.label: Where to apply
facts.value: Submit permit application to the county
facts.note: ADEQ's homeowner page says applicants submit the permit application to the county in which the property is located.

facts.label: Type 4 permit scope
facts.note: ADEQ's Type 4 general permit page says the permit authorizes onsite wastewater treatment facilities from 0 to 24,000 gallons per day.

facts.label: Permit sequence
facts.value: Notice of Intent to Construct, then Notice of Intent to Discharge
facts.note: ADEQ describes a Notice of Intent to Construct, a two-year window to construct, and a Notice of Intent to Discharge before the permit stays valid for the life of the facility.

facts.label: First technical document
facts.note: ADEQ's delegation page points homeowners to delegated county forms and the Uniform Site Investigation Report used by those county programs.

facts.label: Transfer and ownership lookup
facts.note: ADEQ's OWN portal lets users search prior notices of transfer for onsite wastewater treatment facilities.

facts.label: Primary rule context
facts.note: Massachusetts tank-size and septic planning questions are usually entangled with Title 5, not just a generic statewide gallon table.

facts.label: Buyer and seller timing
facts.value: Inspection and transfer timing can change urgency
facts.sourceSection: Buying or selling property with a septic system
facts.note: Massachusetts homeowners often discover septic sizing and replacement questions because a sale or transfer forces the issue through Title 5 timing.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.note: Massachusetts owners usually need the local Board of Health path once they move from a planning estimate into a real upgrade or repair conversation.

facts.label: Primary workflow split
facts.value: DEP-managed counties versus county health path
facts.sourceSection: Statewide onsite sewage and septic overview
facts.note: Florida's first homeowner question is often jurisdiction, not tank size, because the practical path can run through DEP-managed counties or county health departments.

facts.label: Primary permit step
facts.value: Construction approvals matter before trusting the low end
facts.note: Florida's public permit path makes construction approvals visible, which is why a simple tank-size answer can be less useful than jurisdiction and permit context.

facts.label: Buyer-side records risk
facts.value: Homebuyer guide and permit trail matter
facts.note: Florida buyer questions often hinge on records, operating-permit history, and who currently owns the regulatory path.

facts.label: Gravity system cadence
facts.note: Washington is unusually clear that gravity systems are inspected at least every three years, which makes maintenance context more concrete than in many states.

facts.label: Advanced system cadence
facts.note: Washington gives homeowners a concrete split between gravity and advanced systems, which is why inspection, O&M logs, and system type belong in the estimate conversation.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.sourceSection: Management roles for onsite sewage systems
facts.note: Washington homeowners usually need the local health jurisdiction before they need a more elaborate online estimate.

facts.label: Local authority lookup
facts.note: Washington has a strong local lookup path, which supports records-first and permit-first workflow pages.

facts.label: Primary rule context
facts.value: Management program and local oversight risk
facts.note: New Jersey pages work because the state can feel more like an ongoing management and reporting problem than a simple tank-size question.

facts.label: Special-area risk
facts.note: Pinelands context is one reason New Jersey needs state-specific pages instead of a generic national calculator pitch.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: Local board of health or local health department
facts.note: New Jersey homeowners usually need local health review and records next, especially where management ordinances or Pinelands rules apply.

facts.label: Primary permit structure
facts.value: Improvement permit, construction authorization, operation permit
facts.note: North Carolina's permit ladder is one of its strongest differentiators, and it matters more to homeowners than a one-number tank answer.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.note: North Carolina usually turns into a county health department workflow quickly, so local office context belongs next to any sizing estimate.

facts.label: Program context
facts.note: North Carolina homeowner pages are strongest when they explain the permit ladder and county path together.

facts.label: Primary permitting context
facts.value: County or local public health authority
facts.note: Missouri is strong for local workflow content because the path can run through different county or local authorities.

facts.label: County-by-county routing
facts.sourceSection: Onsite sewage permitting jurisdiction by county
facts.note: Missouri's county-by-county authority map is one reason local verification belongs very early in the homeowner workflow.

facts.label: Local public health lookup
facts.value: Official local public health agency directory
facts.note: Missouri homeowners often need the local public health agency before they need more theoretical estimate detail.
